abstract: The geometric structure and electronic configuration of reaction centers are the primary determinants of catalytic performance; however, these sites often undergo dynamic transformations under operating conditions that remain difficult to control. Here, we report a straightforward light-activation strategy to modulate the dynamic restructuring of Cu active sites on a TiO₂-supported catalyst during low-temperature methanol steam reforming. We show that under illumination, photoexcited charge carriers from the TiO₂ support trigger a structural restoration of the catalyst, reverting thermally deactivated Cu₂O back to its highly active metallic Cu state. This light-induced recovery results in a significant boost to both catalytic activity and long-term stability. Under low-intensity solar irradiation, the optimized Cu/TiO₂ catalyst achieves a H₂ production rate of 1724.1 μmol g⁻¹ min⁻¹, surpassing the performance of most traditional photocatalytic and thermocatalytic systems. By exploiting these strong light-matter-reactant interactions, we demonstrate the ability to manipulate Cu active sites in situ, offering a viable pathway for the real-time functionalization and maintenance of catalysts during operation.
